stem: A triangle has two sides of length \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \) with an included angle of \( [[deg]]^\circ \). What is the area of the triangle?
solution: The area of a triangle from two sides and the included angle is \( \tfrac12 ab\sin C = \tfrac12\cdot[[a]]\cdot[[b]]\cdot\sin([[deg]]^\circ) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This omits the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) in the area formula.
This uses \( \tfrac12 ab \) as if \( \sin C = 1 \) (a right angle).
This uses \( \cos C \) in place of \( \sin C \).
This is just the product of the two sides.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by two from the correct area.

stem: In a triangle, side \( a=[[a]] \) is opposite an angle of \( [[degA]]^\circ \), and a second angle measures \( [[degB]]^\circ \). Find the length of the side opposite the \( [[degB]]^\circ \) angle.
solution: By the Law of Sines, \( \dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} \), so \( b = \dfrac{a\sin B}{\sin A} = \dfrac{[[a]]\cdot\sin([[degB]]^\circ)}{\sin([[degA]]^\circ)} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, using \( \sin A/\sin B \) instead of \( \sin B/\sin A \).
This forgets to divide by \( \sin A \).
This uses cosines instead of sines in the Law of Sines ratio.
This just returns the given side length unchanged.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is half the correct value.

stem: In a triangle, side \( a=[[a]] \) is opposite an angle \( A=[[degA]]^\circ \), and a second side has length \( b=[[b]] \). Find the measure (in radians) of the angle \( B \) opposite side \( b \), assuming \( B \) is acute.
solution: By the Law of Sines, \( \dfrac{\sin B}{b} = \dfrac{\sin A}{a} \), so \( \sin B = \dfrac{b\sin A}{a} \) and (taking the acute solution) \( B = \arcsin\dfrac{[[b]]\cdot\sin([[degA]]^\circ)}{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the two sides.
This just returns angle \( A \) unchanged, ignoring the different side.
This uses the obtuse supplement instead of the acute angle.
